audit_status.source_base: Hebrew Joshua / Yehoshua circumcision, Passover, manna cessation, and commander encounter narrative, draft aligned later
audit_status.review_note: First-pass draft reviewed for kings' fear, second circumcision, wilderness-generation explanation, Gilgal naming, Passover, produce of land, manna cessation, and commander of YHWH's host.

verses.restored: It was, when all kings of Amorite who were across Jordan toward sea and all kings of Canaanite who were by sea heard that YHWH dried waters of Jordan before children of Israel until we crossed, that their heart melted and there was no spirit in them anymore because of children of Israel.
At that time YHWH said to Joshua, Make for yourself knives of flint and circumcise again children of Israel a second time.
Joshua made for himself knives of flint and circumcised children of Israel at Gibeath-haaraloth/Hill of Foreskins.
This is reason Joshua circumcised them: all people who came out from Egypt, males, all men of war, died in wilderness on way after they came out from Egypt.
For all people who came out were circumcised, but all people born in wilderness on way after coming out from Egypt they had not circumcised.

For forty years children of Israel walked in wilderness until all nation, men of war who came out from Egypt, came to an end because they did not listen to voice of YHWH; YHWH swore to them not to let them see land YHWH swore to their fathers to give us, land flowing with milk and honey.
Their sons he raised in their place; them Joshua circumcised, for they were uncircumcised because they had not circumcised them on way.
It was, when all nation finished being circumcised, they remained in their place in camp until they recovered.
YHWH said to Joshua, Today I have rolled away reproach of Egypt from upon you. So name of that place is called Gilgal/Rolling to this day.
Children of Israel camped at Gilgal and made Passover on fourteenth day of month in evening, in plains of Jericho.
They ate from produce of land on day after Passover, unleavened bread and roasted grain, on that very day.
Manna ceased on day after they ate from produce of land, and there was no longer manna for children of Israel; they ate from produce of land of Canaan that year.

It was, when Joshua was by Jericho, that he lifted his eyes and saw, and look, a man standing opposite him with drawn sword in his hand. Joshua went to him and said, Are you for us or for our adversaries?
He said, No/Neither; but as commander of host of YHWH I have now come. Joshua fell on his face to earth and bowed and said to him, What does my lord speak to his servant?
Commander of host of YHWH said to Joshua, Remove your sandal from upon your foot, for place where you stand is holy. And Joshua did so.

translation_notes: The chapter opens from the perspective of terrified local kings, making clear that Israel's miracle is experienced by others as approaching military danger.

Circumcision is performed on the wilderness-born male generation as covenant marking. This ancient communal rite must not authorize nonconsensual genital cutting outside lawful, ethical, and culturally grounded practice.
Recovery is explicitly allowed before further movement, recognizing bodily vulnerability after the procedure.
Gilgal is connected by wordplay to rolling away Egypt's reproach, though the exact meaning of that reproach remains debated.
Passover and cessation of manna mark transition from wilderness dependence to eating produce already growing in inhabited land; the labor and presence of prior inhabitants must not be erased.
The commander's answer to Joshua's partisan question is literally 'No' or may imply 'Neither.' Divine holiness refuses easy reduction to 'our side.'
The holy-ground scene echoes Moses at the burning bush while placing Joshua under, not above, Divine command.
safety_note: Do not use Joshua 5 to justify forced genital modification, conquest triumphalism, erasure of prior inhabitants' labor, or claims that Divine power automatically belongs to one's side. Center recovery, covenant continuity, Passover memory, dependence giving way to responsibility, and the commander's refusal of simplistic partisanship.
